1.20: A direct revelation: 'All the promises of God find their Yes in him.' The entire prophetic, Abrahamic, and Davidic promise-system of the OT is ratified in Christ — he is not merely one who keeps promises but the one in whom the promise-structure itself is fulfilled. The 'Yes' is not merely verbal agreement but the constitutive reality of Christ's person and work: the covenant, the Servant mission, the Davidic throne, the new creation — all are 'Yes' in him. This is 2 Corinthians' opening Christological foundation before all the apologetics and ethics that follow.

3.18: A direct revelation: 'We all, with unveiled faces, beholding the glory of the Lord, are being transformed into the same image from one degree of glory to another.' Moses saw YHWH's glory and came away with a fading reflected radiance; in Christ, every believer beholds the glory of God directly (John 1:14: we have seen his glory) and is progressively transformed into that same image. The Christological center: the image we are being transformed into is Christ himself (Rom 8:29: conformed to the image of his Son). The new covenant is therefore the transformation of believers into Christ-likeness — the goal of all Paul's ministry (Gal 4:19: until Christ is formed in you).

4.6: A direct revelation: 'God who said Let light shine out of darkness has shone in our hearts to give the light of the knowledge of the glory of God in the face of Jesus Christ.' The glory of God — YHWH's Shekinah-presence, the consuming fire, the cloud-pillar, the heavenly radiance — is now specifically located: in the face of Jesus Christ. This is Paul's most explicit identification of Jesus with the divine glory. Conversion is a new-creation event (v. 6 echoes Gen 1:3) in which God's glory, once accessible only in the tabernacle and temple, is now encountered in the person of Christ.

5.17: A direct revelation: 'If anyone is in Christ, new creation.' The eschatological new creation that Isaiah promised (Isa 65:17-25) is not merely a future cosmic event but a present reality for every person united to Christ. Christ is the embodiment of the new creation: his resurrection is the firstfruits of the new-creation body (1 Cor 15:20, 45); his Spirit is the down-payment of the new-creation life (2 Cor 1:22; 5:5). 'In Christ' is not merely a forensic status but a participatory location — to be in Christ is to be located within the sphere of the new creation that has already broken into the old age.
5.21: A direct revelation: 'For our sake he made him to be sin who knew no sin, so that in him we might become the righteousness of God.' The great exchange is the Christological ground of all Paul's soteriology: the sinless one (the one who 'knew no sin' in the experiential-relational sense of Heb 4:15) was made to be sin (took sin's full weight and consequence) so that those in him are made to be the righteousness of God. The substitution is complete and symmetrical: my sin became his; his righteousness becomes mine. Paul's atonement theology cannot be understood apart from this verse.

8.9: A direct revelation: 'For you know the grace of our Lord Jesus Christ, that though he was rich, yet for your sake he became poor, so that you by his poverty might become rich.' Paul's appeal for the collection for Jerusalem is grounded in the Incarnation: Christ's voluntary self-emptying (becoming poor) for others is the prototype and motive for the Macedonians' and Corinthians' voluntary financial sacrifice for others. The Christological movement (rich → poor → rich for others) is the template for Christian generosity. This is the ethical application of the Christ-hymn logic (Phil 2:5-11): the self-giving of Christ generates the self-giving of his people.

12.9: A direct revelation: 'My grace is sufficient for you, for my power is made perfect in weakness.' The risen Christ speaks directly to Paul in response to three petitions. The Christological principle being enacted: Christ's power is not supplementary to human strength but is maximally displayed where human strength is absent. This is the Christological logic of the cross applied to Christian experience: just as God's power was manifested most fully in the apparent weakness of crucifixion, so Christ's power is manifested most fully through the apostle's weakness. The cross is not a one-time exception but the ongoing pattern of divine power-in-weakness.
